comments.text: I have written a short note on this problem: GitHub repository With the assistance of GPT-5.5 Pro, I found a proof of a relative independence result. Assuming that Lebesgue measure extends to a countably additive measure on all subsets of the real line, the answer is positive: every family $(A_y)_{y\in\mathbb R}$ with $m^*(A_y)< 1$ admits an infinite independent set. The proof uses Fremlin's Theorem 543C, a Fubini-type inequality attributed there to a 1970 note of Kunen. I have checked the argument myself, but I am not an expert in this area. Comments, corrections, and references to related work would be very welcome.

Congrats. I ran standard check which found no issues. This is a screening and is not meant to be comprehensive. Note that I am not a set theorist. The main result seems to be that if $\text{Con}(\mathsf{ZFC} + \mathsf{FMEA})$ holds, then the first question is independent of $\mathsf{ZFC}$.

I have uploaded v2. The main difference from the previous version is that the appeal to Fremlin 543C (Kunen's theorem) has been replaced with a direct, elementary proof of the needed section inequality. (Assisted by GPT-5.5 Pro)

According to the problem's description, I think the problem status should be updated. 1. [He72] showed that the answer to the first question is no, assuming CH. Therefore this question should get the NOT PROVABLE status. 2. [NPS87] proved that if the sets $A_x$ are closed, there exists an infinite independent set (Corollary 1). This strictly satisfies the requirement for a set of size 3, so this question should get the PROVED status.

When the problem has multiple components, I am taking the status of the joint problem to be the intersection of the statuses of the components (i.e., the strongest status that applies to all components). Unfortunately, the intersection of NOT PROVABLE and PROVED is still OPEN.

Thanks, I had misread the result of [NPS87], so didn't realise their result proved the second question. The only remaining question is really whether the first question is provable/true without the continuum hypothesis. Whether or not Hechler's result answers the spirit of the question is delicate I think, and best answered by an expert in the area.
Just a minor typo: the second line says "the the second question." (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

@BorisAlexeev - thanks for this. If I'm reading these papers right, it looks like: (a) Newelski, Pawlikowski, and Seredyński had the assumption of both closed and bounded, so not quite settling the first part. (b) Hechler's paper is for the first question, showing that it is false assuming the continuum hypothesis. Hechler's paper also had a separate theorem concerning part (A), but another result in that same paper does indeed address part (C) in the manner stated here, although Komjáth does not seem to record this.

The paper "The Erdős-Hajnal problem list" by Péter Komjáth (available online) includes this as Problem 38. I believe that Hechler's paper is for part (A), which is not included on this page. I also saw similar statements in another paper. In other words, I believe that Hechler's paper does not address the first problem here. The first problem here is part (C) and the theorem of (Newelski–Pawlikowski–Seredyński is stated there. The second problem here is part (B).

Someone has a link to the paper of Hechler's paper? Since if both Hechler's paper and the one of Newelski, Pawlikowski and Seredyński is correct, it would imply that the continuum hypothesis is not correct?!

I believe that the first question is resolved (positively) in the paper "Infinite Free Set for Small Measure Set Mappings" by Ludomir Newelski, Janusz Pawlikowski and Witold Seredyński. Here's a link to the journal article. I believe that "Bemerkungen über die Unabhängigkeit der Punkte in Bezug auf mengenwertige Funktionen" by S. Gładysz is likely the reference mentioned above with respect to the second question? Here's a link to the journal article. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
